title: Inverting a Cylinder Through Isometric Immersions and Isometric Embeddings
overall_summary: Halpern and Weaver study when a flat right circular cylinder can be turned inside out through isometric immersions or embeddings, and when a flat Möbius strip admits smooth isometric immersions or embeddings in R3. Their main cylinder result is that the inclusion of C_h = S1 × [-h,h] is isometrically homotopic to its inside-out version if and only if h < 1, with an explicit embedding isotopy for h < π/(π+2). Their main Möbius immersion result is sharp: M_l = ([0,l] × [0,1])/(0,y)~(l,1-y) isometrically immerses in R3 if and only if l > π/2. The paper also proves that embeddings are strictly harder than immersions, gives a non-sharp lower bound l > π/2 + ε for embedded Möbius strips, and gives the classical Sadowsky-type construction showing M_l isometrically embeds for l > √3. The core techniques are ruled-surface/generating-line structure of flat isometric immersions, a total-curvature estimate for antiparallel generating lines, a cohomological invariant built from set-valued families of generating directions, and a pseudofolding method that smooths explicit paper-folding patterns. For the original clean-triangulation problem, the most directly relevant content is the √3 Möbius embedding construction, the pseudofolding/fold-pattern method, and the total-curvature/generating-line obstruction strategy.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorems 2.1 and 2.2: mod-2 frame invariant for h < 1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let C_h = S1 × [-h,h] with its flat metric induced by the standard inclusion in R3, and let h < 1. For an isometric immersion f: C_h → R3, let N(p) be a unit normal to f at (p,0), and let T(p) be the unit tangent vector to the central curve f restricted to S1 × {0}. Define a loop γ: S1 → SO(3) by γ(p) = (T(p), N(p), T(p) × N(p)), viewed as an oriented orthonormal frame. The homotopy class G(f) = [γ] in π1(SO(3)) ≅ Z2 is invariant under isometric homotopy. If f has product form f = y × j: S1 × [-h,h] → R2 × R = R3, where y: S1 → R2 is a smooth closed unit-speed curve and j: [-h,h] → R is the inclusion, then G(f) is the tangent winding number of y reduced modulo 2.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For h < 1 the invariant is defined directly from the frame along the central circle. An isometric homotopy f_t gives a continuous homotopy of the corresponding frame loops γ_t in SO(3), so the element of π1(SO(3)) is constant. In the product case y × j, the normal and tangent frame loop is exactly the standard frame determined by the tangent direction of the plane curve y together with the vertical direction; its class in π1(SO(3)) is the winding number of the tangent map S1 → S1, reduced mod 2 under the isomorphism π1(SO(3)) ≅ Z2.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 3: cohomology of allowable set-valued maps
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let F: X → 2^Y be a set-valued map between Hausdorff spaces. It is called allowable if each value F(x) is nonempty and contractible, and the graph gr F = {(x,y) in X × Y : y in F(x)} is compact. For an allowable map F, define an induced homomorphism F*: H^q(Y) → H^q(X) in Alexander cohomology with integer coefficients as follows. Let P_X: gr F → X and P_Y: gr F → Y be the coordinate projections. Since every fiber P_X^{-1}(x) = {x} × F(x) is contractible, the Vietoris-Begle theorem implies P_X*: H^q(X) → H^q(gr F) is an isomorphism. Set F* = (P_X*)^{-1} ∘ P_Y*. This construction agrees with the ordinary induced map for a single-valued continuous map, is compatible with precomposition by a continuous single-valued map, and is invariant under allowable homotopy. Moreover, if F and G are allowable and F(x) ⊂ G(x) for all x, then F and G induce the same cohomology homomorphism because they are allowable-homotopic.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The key input is the Vietoris-Begle mapping theorem applied to P_X: gr F → X. The hypotheses hold because gr F is compact Hausdorff and the fibers x × F(x) have trivial reduced cohomology by contractibility. Thus P_X* is invertible and the displayed definition of F* makes sense. For a single-valued map f, gr F is homeomorphic to X and the formula reduces to f*. For precomposition g: Z → X, the graph of F ∘ g is compared with gr F by the map (z,y) ↦ (g(z),y), and the projection diagrams commute, giving (F ∘ g)* = g* ∘ F*. Homotopy invariance follows by applying the precomposition rule to the inclusions X × {0} and X × {1}, which are homotopic. If F(x) ⊂ G(x), the paper defines a piecewise constant allowable homotopy H(x,t) equal to F(x) for t < 1 and G(x) at t = 1, whose graph is compact because it is the union of gr F × I and gr G × {1}; hence F* = G*.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.4: upper semicontinuity of allowable maps
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: If F: X → 2^Y is allowable, x is a point of X, and V is an open subset of Y containing F(x), then there exists a neighborhood U of x in X such that F(x') ⊂ V for every x' in U.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is the compact-graph upper semicontinuity property. If no such U existed, one could find a net or sequence x_i → x and points y_i in F(x_i) outside V. Compactness of gr F gives a convergent subnet (x_i,y_i) → (x,y) in gr F. Then y lies in F(x) ⊂ V, contradicting that the complement of V contains all y_i and is closed after passing to a suitable subnet outside V.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 4.1: structure of generating line segments for flat cylinder immersions
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let f: C_h → R3 be an isometric immersion, where C_h = S1 × [-h,h]. Let π: R × [-h,h] → C_h be the universal covering map and set g = f ∘ π. For s in R define V_g(s) to be the set of all real t such that the endpoints (s-t,-h) and (s+t,h) are mapped by g at their intrinsic Euclidean distance, i.e. ||g(s+t,h) - g(s-t,-h)|| = ||(s+t,h) - (s-t,-h)||. Equivalently, the straight segment joining those two points in the covering strip is mapped rigidly. For p in S1, define V(p) = V_g(s), where π(s,0) = (p,0). Then: (a) for each p, V(p) is a nonempty closed line segment; (b) the graph of V and the image of V are compact; (c) there exist monotone nondecreasing functions T,B: R → R satisfying T(s+2π) = T(s)+2π, B(s+2π) = B(s)+2π, (T(s)+B(s))/2 = s, and T(s)-s in V_g(s) for all s. In addition, if a, β are real numbers and t in V_g(a), t' in V_g(β) are chosen so that the corresponding two cross-strip line segments do not cross in the covering strip, then T and B may be chosen with T(a)-a = t and T(β)-β = t'.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof uses standard facts about smooth isometric immersions of flat surfaces: through each interior point of nonzero mean curvature there is a unique straight line segment lying on the surface; when extended, it remains on the surface until it hits the boundary, and nonzero mean curvature persists along it. Applied to g on the covering strip, this line segment through (s,0) cannot be horizontal, because a horizontal ruling would be periodic and contradict immersion/covering behavior. Hence, at every central point with nonzero mean curvature, there is a unique segment from bottom boundary to top boundary, giving a singleton V_g(s). At boundary points of the nonzero-mean-curvature set, take monotone limits of nearby top and bottom endpoints; monotonicity is forced because crossing rulings would contradict uniqueness of rulings on the flat surface. On open intervals where the mean curvature along the central curve vanishes, the central curve maps as a straight segment; using the already-defined endpoint rulings at the ends of such an interval, elementary Euclidean geometry shows that the whole quadrilateral bounded by the two endpoint rulings and top and bottom boundary intervals is mapped rigidly. In such a quadrilateral, V_g(s) is a closed interval and T,B can be defined by linear interpolation. Periodicity comes from the covering translation. Compactness of the graph follows by first observing that all t in all V(p) are uniformly bounded: otherwise endpoints in the compact cylinder would map too far apart. Closedness follows directly from the defining equality, and compactness of the image is its projection.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 4.2: compactness of generating-line data in homotopies
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let H: C_h × I → R3 be a homotopy through isometric immersions. For each time τ in I, define V_{H_τ} as in Proposition 4.1, and define V_H(p,τ) = V_{H_τ}(p). Then the graph of V_H and the image of V_H are compact.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is the same compactness argument as in Proposition 4.1, with the homotopy parameter included. The cylinder C_h and the interval of parameters are compact, so the image of H is uniformly bounded. This gives a uniform bound on all possible t-values in V_H. Closedness of the graph follows from continuity of H and the distance equality defining V, and hence the graph is compact.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 5.1: antiparallel generating lines are separated by more than πh
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let f: C_h → R3 be an isometric immersion, let g: R × [-h,h] → R3 be its lift, and let V_g(s) be as in Proposition 4.1. Suppose a < β, t in V_g(a), and t' in V_g(β). If the two generating vectors are antiparallel in the sense that g(a+t,h) - g(a-t,-h) = λ(g(β+t',h) - g(β-t',-h)) for some λ < 0, then β - a > πh.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Using Proposition 4.1, choose monotone functions T,B with T(a)-a=t and T(β)-β=t'. Define the top and bottom curves in R3 by Tbar(s)=g(T(s),h) and Bbar(s)=g(B(s),-h), and define φ(s)=Tbar(s)-Bbar(s). For every s, the segment from (B(s),-h) to (T(s),h) is mapped rigidly, so ||φ(s)|| = sqrt((T(s)-B(s))^2 + (2h)^2) ≥ 2h. The antiparallel hypothesis says φ(a) and φ(β) lie on opposite rays through the origin. Any curve in R3 staying outside the open ball of radius 2h and joining a ray to the opposite ray has length at least the semicircle length 2πh. Thus length(φ|[a,β]) ≥ 2πh. On the other hand, length(φ) ≤ length(Tbar) + length(Bbar), and because g is isometric and T,B are monotone, these lengths are T(β)-T(a) and B(β)-B(a). With T(a)=a+t, T(β)=β+t', B(a)=a-t, and B(β)=β-t', their sum is 2(β-a). Hence β-a ≥ πh. The paper then rules out equality: equality would force ||φ(s)||=2h throughout, hence T(s)=B(s)=s, and force equality in the triangle inequality, so the top and bottom derivatives are oppositely directed. This makes the derivative of the central curve vanish, contradicting that g is an isometric immersion. Therefore β-a > πh.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 5.2: lower bound for isometric immersion of a flat Möbius strip
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M_l = ([0,l] × [0,1])/(0,y)~(l,1-y) be the flat Möbius strip of length l and width 1. If M_l admits a smooth isometric immersion into R3, then l > π/2.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper notes that this follows either directly from the proof of Proposition 5.1 or by passing to the double cover of the Möbius strip by a flat cylinder. In the double cover, the Möbius identification reverses the transverse direction, so a generating line and its translate by one Möbius length become antiparallel. Applying Proposition 5.1 with half-width h = 1/2 forces the horizontal separation l to be greater than πh = π/2.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 6.1: deformation retract of the space Q
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let e3 = (0,0,1) in R3, let R = {t e3 : t < 0} be the downward open ray, and define Q = {(u,v) in (R3 \ R) × R3 : u and v are linearly independent}. Embed S1 in Q by j(p) = (e3,p), viewing S1 as the unit circle in the xy-plane. Then j(S1) is a strong deformation retract of Q.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: First apply Gram-Schmidt to deform any pair (u,v) in Q to (u/||u||, v_perp/||v_perp||), where the second vector is the normalized component of v perpendicular to u. Then rotate the first vector u/||u|| to e3 along the shorter geodesic arc on S2; the excluded downward ray ensures this can be done continuously without ambiguity. Apply the same rotation to the second vector. The result is a pair (e3,p) with p in the xy-plane unit circle. Points already of the form (e3,p) remain fixed, giving a strong deformation retract.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Sections 7-8: integer invariant G(f) for h > 1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let h > 1 and let f: C_h → R3 be an isometric immersion. With g, V_g, and the generating vectors as above, define W(p) to be the set of image vectors from the center point f(p,0) to the top endpoint of a generating segment through (p,0). Choose w in W(0) and a rotation U in SO(3) taking w to a positive multiple of e3. Let T(p) be the unit tangent to the central curve. Define a set-valued map φ: S1 → 2^Q by φ(p) = U(W(p),T(p)), where Q is the space in Lemma 6.1. Then φ is allowable. Let η be a generator of H^1(S1;Z), and define G(f) in Z by φ* j*^{-1}(η) = G(f)η, where j: S1 → Q is the deformation-retract inclusion. The integer G(f) is independent of the choices of w and U. If f = y × j0: S1 × [-h,h] → R2 × R = R3 is a product isometric immersion with y a smooth closed unit-speed plane curve and j0 the interval inclusion, then G(f) is the tangent winding number of y.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The key point is that, when h > 1, Proposition 5.1 prevents any generating vector from becoming antiparallel to the chosen vector w within one period: antiparallel generating lines would have to be separated by more than πh > π, but on a circle one can choose representatives separated by at most π. Thus, after the rotation U, the first component avoids the forbidden downward ray R, so φ takes values in Q. Proposition 4.1 gives that each W(p) is a nonempty closed line segment and that the graph is compact; hence φ is allowable. Lemma 6.1 identifies H^1(Q) with Z through j, allowing the definition of G(f). Independence of U follows because the rotations carrying w to the positive e3-axis form a path-connected circle, and varying U along a path gives an allowable homotopy of set-valued maps. Independence of w follows because W(0) is a line segment; moving w linearly to another choice w' and choosing compatible rotations again gives an allowable homotopy. In the product case, the generating set is vertical and the resulting map to Q retracts to the tangent map of the plane curve y, so the integer is exactly the tangent winding number.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 8.1: local constancy of G(f)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let h > 1 and let f: C_h → R3 be an isometric immersion. Define a closeness measure between isometric immersions f and f' by p(f,f') = sup_{x in C_h} ||f(x)-f'(x)|| + sup_{s in R} ||∂_s(g(s,0)-g'(s,0))||, where g and g' are the lifted maps to R × [-h,h]. Then there exists δ > 0 such that every isometric immersion f' with p(f,f') < δ satisfies G(f') = G(f).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is a careful stability argument for the allowable set-valued map φ. First, Lemma 8.2 is an elementary compactness lemma controlling zero sets of nonnegative functions under small perturbations. Lemma 8.3 applies it to the defining distance defect for V_g(s), showing that if the graph of φ_f lies in an open set M, then for f' sufficiently close the graph of φ_{f'} also lies in M. Lemma 8.4 similarly shows W_{f'}(0) lies in a small neighborhood of W_f(0). Lemma 8.5 gives a uniform ε-neighborhood of the image of φ_f that remains inside Q after any rotation usable in the definition of G(f). Next, using upper semicontinuity of allowable maps, the graph of φ_f is enclosed in the interior of a piecewise constant allowable map ψ: S1 → 2^{R3×R3} with convex values and image contained in a small neighborhood of φ_f. For f' close, after choosing a rotation U' compatible with a vector w' in W_{f'}(0), one has φ_{f'}(p) ⊂ ψ(p), while φ_f(p) ⊂ ψ(p) as well. Since inclusion of allowable maps induces homotopy, φ_f, ψ, and φ_{f'} have the same induced cohomology map. Therefore G(f') = G(f).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 8.6 and Corollary 8.7: winding obstruction to cylinder inversion for h > 1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let y × j and y' × j be product isometric immersions S1 × [-h,h] → R2 × R = R3, where y,y' are smooth closed unit-speed plane curves. If these product immersions are isometrically homotopic and h > 1, then the tangent winding numbers of y and y' are equal. If h < 1, then their tangent winding numbers are equal modulo 2. Consequently, if h > 1, the standard inclusion i: C_h → R3 is not isometrically homotopic to the inside-out map r(x,y,z) = (x,y,-z).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For h > 1, use the integer invariant G(f) of Sections 7-8, its homotopy invariance from Theorem 8.1, and the product formula G(y×j)=winding(y). For h < 1, use the mod-2 frame invariant of Section 2. For the corollary, rotate the inside-out map r through an ambient rotation to a product map whose plane curve is the reflection of the unit circle, so its tangent winding number is -1, while the inclusion has winding number +1. Since +1 and -1 are distinct integers, h > 1 forbids an isometric homotopy.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 9.1: unit of pseudofolding
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix a strip of half-width h. For each positive strip length l, there is a smooth isotopy ψ: [0,l] × [-h,h] × [0,θ(l)] → R3 through isometric embeddings, written ψ_t(x,y)=ψ(x,y,t), with the following properties. Initially ψ_0(x,y)=(x,y,0). A front margin x ≤ ε is fixed throughout. A back margin x ≥ l-ε undergoes, up to a time-dependent translation T_t, a rotation R_t about the original x-axis by angle t radians: ψ_t(x,y)=T_t ∘ R_t(x,y) there. The middle portion stays horizontally between the two margins. For all points and times, if ψ_t(x,y)=(a,b,c), then sqrt(b^2+c^2) ≤ h + l e(l), where e(l) → 0 as l → 0. Finally, the total twist angle satisfies θ(l) = (1 + A(l)) l/h with A(l) → 0 as l → 0.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The authors first construct a pseudofold: choose a smooth family of unit-speed plane curves γ_t that agree with a straight line outside a small interval and whose curvature is a scaled bump with total turning t. Crossing γ_t with a line gives a smooth isometric bending of a planar strip that approximates an actual fold along a crease line; the small curved neighborhood is the fudge factor. A unit of pseudofolding is built from three such pseudofolds along three pseudocrease lines arranged as in Figure 9.3. The first and third pseudofolds are assigned equal angles t. The middle pseudofold angle θ_2(t) is chosen smoothly so that the image of a central rigid segment in the left construction matches the corresponding segment in the right construction after a rotation about the x-axis and a translation. This matching is verified by comparing the projected directions of the segment and the surface normals. Reparameterizing the homotopy by the resulting rotation angle gives the stated back-margin twist. As the fudge factor tends to zero, the construction converges to the ideal folded pattern in Figure 9.1, which gives the estimates: the motion stays inside a cylinder of radius h + o(l), and the twist achieved per unit length is asymptotically 1/h.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorems 10.2 and 10.3: cylinder inversion by pseudofolding
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let i: C_h → R3 be the standard inclusion and r: C_h → R3 be the inside-out map r(x,y,z)=(x,y,-z). Then i is isometrically homotopic to r if and only if h < 1. Moreover, if h < π/(π+2), then i is isometrically isotopic to r through isometric embeddings.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The necessity for homotopy when h > 1 is Corollary 8.7. For the constructive direction, the paper describes an explicit inversion using the pseudofolding unit of Lemma 9.1. The cylinder is viewed as a flexible tape. Starting from the standard cylinder, an accordion-like folded region is created. Two pseudofolding machines feed tape into banks of completed pseudofolding units while an accordion rotates through 180 degrees. After the midway position, the process is reversed on the other side to obtain the inside-out cylinder. In an immersion homotopy, the two banks of completed units may pass through each other, so the only essential length cost is two banks each requiring asymptotic length πh; the circumference 2π must exceed 2πh, giving h < 1. For an embedding isotopy, extra length is needed to keep two accordions and two banks disjoint. The paper gives the length requirement 2π > 2h + 2h + πh + πh, equivalent to h < π/(π+2). The pseudofolding estimates ensure that, by using sufficiently small units, the ideal folded motion can be approximated by smooth isometric embeddings or immersions with the same length inequalities.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 11.1: classification of product isometric immersions up to isometric homotopy
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let y × j and y' × j be product isometric immersions S1 × [-h,h] → R2 × R = R3, where y,y': S1 → R2 are smooth closed unit-speed plane curves and j is the inclusion of [-h,h] in R. If h > 1, then y × j and y' × j are isometrically homotopic if and only if y and y' have the same tangent winding number. If h < 1, then they are isometrically homotopic if and only if their tangent winding numbers are equal modulo 2.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The only-if direction is Proposition 8.6. For the if direction with h > 1, use the Whitney-Graustein theorem: two regular closed plane curves are regularly homotopic if and only if they have the same tangent winding number. Given a regular homotopy between y and y', rescale each intermediate curve and reparameterize by arclength to obtain an isometric homotopy of unit-speed curves; crossing with the interval inclusion gives the desired isometric homotopy of cylinders. For h < 1, it remains to show that the winding number can be changed by ±2 through an isometric homotopy. The authors first homotope y to a curve y' with a long circular arc of length exceeding 2πh and a small leftover segment. On the corresponding cylindrical part, they apply the cylinder inversion construction of Sections 9-10 while holding the leftover segment fixed. This reflects the circular arc and changes the tangent winding by 2 or -2. Repeating this move reduces the problem to the equal-winding case.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 12.1: sharp smooth isometric immersion criterion for flat Möbius strips
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M_l = ([0,l] × [0,1])/(0,y)~(l,1-y) be the flat Möbius strip of length l and width 1. Then M_l admits a smooth isometric immersion into R3 if and only if l > π/2.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The lower bound l > π/2 is Corollary 5.2. For the upper construction, the paper divides a strip of paper into three rectangular sections. In the first rectangle ABCD, draw an odd number n of pseudocrease lines, each making a small angle w with the vertical, chosen so that n·2w = π. Pseudofolding alternately back and forth along these lines brings the directed edge CD into a position antiparallel to AB. As the gaps between successive pseudocreases and the fudge factors tend to zero, the folded rectangle approaches an ideal folded rectangle in which CD lands on AB, so D can be made arbitrarily close to A. The second rectangle CDFE has two parallel pseudocrease lines; by choosing the folding angle, EF is brought to the position of BA translated perpendicularly. The third rectangle EFHG is then used to join GH smoothly to BA, completing the Möbius identification. By taking n large and the gaps and fudge factors small, the required length of the first rectangle approaches π/2 while the extra lengths in the second and third rectangles can be made arbitrarily small. Thus every l > π/2 is achieved.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 13: near-critical inversions must have many near-folds
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let S: C_h × I → R3 be an isometric homotopy from the inclusion i to the inside-out map r, with h close to 1. At some intermediate time τ, the immersion f = S_τ has a pair of antiparallel generating lines. Choosing lifted parameters a < β with β-a ≤ π and using the notation of Proposition 5.1, one obtains β-a > πh and hence β-a is close to π when h is close to 1. For the associated top and bottom endpoint functions T,B and vector φ(s)=g(T(s),h)-g(B(s),-h), the paper derives the following near-equality consequences as h → 1: ||φ(s)|| is uniformly close to 2h; φ(s) stays close to a fixed plane P through φ(a), φ((a+β)/2), and φ(β); outside a set of arbitrarily small measure, φ'(s) is nearly perpendicular to φ(s) and nearly lies in P on a fixed side. Also T(s)-B(s) is uniformly small, T'(s)+B'(s)=2, T',B' ≥ 0, ∫_a^β |T'(s)-B'(s)| ds ≥ 2πh, and |T'(s)-B'(s)| is near 2 for most s. Consequently, along any subinterval of nontrivial size, T'-B' spends roughly half the time near +2 and half near -2. One can choose an arbitrarily long sequence a < s1 < ... < sN < β such that the surface normals near (s_i,0) alternate sign; the central curve is nearly stationary, has high curvature distributed densely, and the boundary curves nearly lie on a circle of radius h.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The starting point is the invariant obstruction: during an inversion, the generating-line data must pass through a configuration with antiparallel generating lines. Proposition 5.1 gives β-a > πh, while periodicity gives β-a ≤ π, so all inequalities used in Proposition 5.1 are nearly equalities for h close to 1. Nearly minimal length of φ outside the ball of radius 2h forces φ to run close to a semicircle in a plane and forces ||φ|| close to 2h. Since the surface is flat and the normal is constant along a straight ruling, the derivative ∂g/∂s is constant along each generating line. This gives the identity φ'(s)=∂_s g(s,0)(T'(s)-B'(s)). The relations (T+B)/2=s, T',B'≥0 imply T'+B'=2 and |T'-B'|≤2. The total-curvature lower bound gives ∫|T'-B'|≥2πh, so for h close to 1 the absolute value is almost always maximal. But T-B remains small, so the signed integral of T'-B' over subintervals is small, forcing oscillation between +2 and -2. These alternating regimes imply alternating tangent directions and surface normals, which is interpreted as many near-folds. The paper states that the simplifying assumption that T and B are continuously differentiable can be removed as in Proposition 5.1.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 14.1: bounded mean curvature prevents near-critical inversion
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For each B > 0, the supremum of h such that C_h can be inverted through isometric immersions whose mean curvatures are bounded in absolute value by B is strictly less than 1.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Section 13 shows that any inversion with h sufficiently close to 1 must pass through a stage where the central curve is almost stationary but has very many near-folds, with tangent direction changing by nearly π over arbitrarily small parameter intervals. This forces arbitrarily large curvature of the central curve. For a flat isometric immersion of the cylinder, this central-curve curvature is equivalent to a lower bound on the surface mean curvature at corresponding points. Therefore a uniform mean-curvature bound B rules out inversions for all h sufficiently close to 1.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 15.1: embedded Möbius strips require length strictly larger than π/2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: There exists ε > 0 such that if the flat Möbius strip M_l = ([0,l] × [0,1])/(0,y)~(l,1-y) admits a smooth isometric embedding into R3, then l > π/2 + ε.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The near-critical analysis of Section 13 applies to an immersion of M_l when l is close to π/2. Choose coordinates so that the plane P from that analysis is the xy-plane. For l sufficiently close to π/2, the generating line segments are nearly horizontal, their centers are all nearly at one point, and the generating vector φ(t+1/2) is nearly at right angles to φ(t). Hence the projections to the xy-plane of two generating segments E = [B(t),T(t)] and F = [B(t+1/2),T(t+1/2)] intersect in a unique point p. Let q in E and r in F be the points lying over p in R3, and define v(t) as the vertical distance from q to r. If the immersion is an embedding, then q and r are never the same point, so v(t) ≠ 0 for all t. The function v(t) is continuous on [0,1/2], so its sign is constant. But by the Möbius identification and the choice of paired segments, v(0) = -v(1/2), contradiction. Thus no embedding exists for l sufficiently close to π/2.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 15: Sadowsky-type upper construction for embedded Möbius strips
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: The paper states that a paper Möbius strip can be made by folding the strip shown in Figure 15.1 along the indicated fold lines, and that using this fold pattern together with the pseudofold technique implies that M_l isometrically embeds in R3 whenever l > √3. The paper notes that M. Sadowsky gave a similar embedding.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The text gives the construction by reference to Figure 15.1 rather than by a full coordinate proof. The intended argument is the same smoothing principle used earlier: start with an explicit embedded polyhedral paper model made by folding a flat strip along the indicated creases; the model has critical length √3. Replace the sharp folds by pseudofolds as in Lemma 9.1, with sufficiently small fudge factors, to obtain smooth isometric embeddings. The smoothing requires an arbitrarily small extra amount of length, hence every l > √3 is realized. The paper presents this as an easy consequence of the displayed fold pattern and the pseudofolding machinery.

proof_techniques.name: Generating-line analysis for flat isometric immersions
proof_techniques.description: The paper repeatedly uses the fact that a smooth flat surface in R3 is ruled away from zero-mean-curvature planar regions. For a lifted cylinder g: R × [-h,h] → R3, this yields line segments running from one boundary component to the other that are mapped rigidly. The set of all such segments through a central point is encoded by V(s), and Proposition 4.1 proves that V(s) is always a compact interval and can be parameterized by monotone top and bottom endpoint functions T(s), B(s).
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original problem uses affine maps on clean triangles with vertices on the boundary of a strip. A discrete analogue of generating lines may be the non-boundary edges of the clean triangles, especially because squeeze maps lengthen the two non-boundary edges and shorten the boundary edge.

proof_techniques.name: Total-curvature estimate from antiparallel rulings
proof_techniques.description: Proposition 5.1 converts the existence of two antiparallel generating vectors into a quantitative separation bound. The vector φ(s) joining bottom to top endpoints stays outside a ball of radius 2h; if its endpoints lie on opposite rays, it must have length at least the semicircle length 2πh. But the length of φ is bounded above by the sum of lengths of the top and bottom endpoint curves, equal to 2(β-a). This gives β-a > πh.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A lower-bound proof for realized β may try to force an antiparallel or crossing configuration in the quotient Möbius strip and then bound horizontal separation by a total-curvature or turning argument. The clean-triangle setting may require a polygonal version of the same estimate, replacing curve length by sums over edges.

proof_techniques.name: Set-valued cohomological invariant
proof_techniques.description: For h > 1, the family of generating directions need not be single-valued, so the paper builds a cohomology class from an allowable set-valued map φ: S1 → 2^Q. The values are contractible line segments, the graph is compact, and the Vietoris-Begle theorem gives a well-defined pullback in cohomology. After normalizing one generating vector, Proposition 5.1 ensures that all values avoid a forbidden ray, so the map lands in a space Q deformation-retracting to S1. The resulting integer G(f) is locally constant and hence homotopy invariant.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the clean triangulation gives multiple possible diagonals or rulings through a point, a set-valued invariant may avoid choosing a ruling continuously. This is useful when combinatorics change along the strip or under limiting arguments.

proof_techniques.name: Pseudofolding: smoothing an origami fold pattern
proof_techniques.description: A pseudofold replaces an ideal crease by a narrow smooth region whose curvature is a bump function with prescribed total turning. It is constructed from a plane curve with compactly supported curvature and crossed with a line. Several pseudofolds can be composed along prescribed pseudocrease lines to approximate a folded paper model while remaining a smooth isometric embedding. The estimates in Lemma 9.1 quantify how much twist is produced per unit length and how close the motion stays to the ideal folded model.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original problem is already piecewise affine, so one may not need to smooth folds; nevertheless, the fold-pattern part of pseudofolding is directly relevant for constructing maps on clean triangulations. The technique explains how a local triangular or crease pattern can accumulate a desired half-twist with controlled length.

proof_techniques.name: Length accounting by accumulated twist
proof_techniques.description: In the cylinder inversion construction, each small pseudofolding unit of length l produces twist asymptotic to l/h. A 180-degree rotation therefore costs about πh length. The global construction becomes a bookkeeping problem: count how many banks of pseudofolding units and accordions are needed, and compare their total length to the circumference 2π.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For the quotient strip in the original problem, β is the available length around the Möbius direction. A proof of the upper bound can similarly be organized by giving a repeatable local unit and summing its required horizontal length to reach a half-twist.

proof_techniques.name: Sharp immersion lower bound via double cover of the Möbius strip
proof_techniques.description: The Möbius immersion lower bound l > π/2 is obtained by passing to the double-covering cylinder and observing that the Möbius flip turns a generating line into an antiparallel one after one strip length. Proposition 5.1 then applies with half-width h = 1/2.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original quotient by (x,y) ↦ (x+β,1-y) is exactly a flat Möbius band. Many arguments may become simpler on the double cover, where the flip becomes a relation between data separated by β.

proof_techniques.name: Near-equality analysis of the total-curvature bound
proof_techniques.description: When h is close to the critical value 1, Proposition 5.1 is nearly sharp. The paper exploits equality cases in every step of the proof: φ must nearly trace a semicircle of radius 2h, T-B must be small, and T'-B' must alternate between nearly +2 and nearly -2. This forces many near-folds and large curvature.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For an optimal √3 lower bound, one may need to analyze near-extremal configurations rather than just prove a coarse obstruction. The paper shows how to mine equality conditions in a length/turning inequality for structural information.

proof_techniques.name: Embedding obstruction by vertical crossing sign
proof_techniques.description: Theorem 15.1 distinguishes embeddings from immersions near the π/2 threshold. Near criticality, two projected generating segments must cross in the xy-plane. For an embedding, the vertical separation at their crossing cannot vanish and hence has a locally constant sign. The Möbius symmetry forces the sign to reverse over half a period, yielding a contradiction.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original realized condition includes injectivity modulo the deck transformation. A discrete version of this sign-change obstruction could rule out short embedded or quotient-injective configurations by forcing projected crossings with inconsistent over-under data.

proof_techniques.name: Sadowsky fold pattern plus smoothing
proof_techniques.description: The paper uses a displayed fold pattern, attributed as similar to Sadowsky's, to produce embedded Möbius strips for every l > √3. The construction is polyhedral at the ideal level and becomes smooth by replacing sharp folds with pseudofolds. The strict inequality l > √3 appears because smoothing the ideal creases requires arbitrarily small extra length.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is the paper's most direct source for the √3 upper construction. In the clean-triangulation setting, the ideal fold pattern may be usable without smoothing, provided its triangular faces and edge-length inequalities can be matched to the required squeeze maps.

other_useful_info: The paper explicitly conjectures that M_l isometrically embeds in R3 only if l > √3; this is the natural counterpart to its Sadowsky-type upper construction and was not proved in the paper. It also conjectures that there is ε > 0 such that the cylinder inclusion is isometrically isotopic to its inside-out version only if h < 1-ε. The cited related works most relevant to these methods are Sadowsky [7] and Wunderlich [11] on developable Möbius bands, Hartman-Nirenberg [5] and Chern-Lashof [2] on flat immersed surfaces and total curvature, Whitney [10] on regular homotopy of plane curves, and Barr [1, Chapter 3], which the paper says gives isometric but nonsmooth embeddings of arbitrarily short Möbius strips.
why_relevant: This is the original Halpern-Weaver paper containing the classical pi/2 lower bound, the sqrt(3) upper construction, and the conjecture solved later by Schwartz. Its homotopy and total-curvature methods are foundational for any proof of an optimal aspect-ratio statement.
